audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 9, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Jonathan covenant memory, Mephibosheth's disability, dynastic fear, land restoration, compulsory court residence, Ziba's household labor, and demeaning self-language.

verses.restored: David said, Is anyone still left from house of Saul, that I may do covenant-kindness with him for Jonathan's sake?
There was a servant from Saul's house named Ziba. They called him to David, and king said, Are you Ziba? He said, Your servant.
King said, Is there no one still from house of Saul, that I may do kindness of Divine with him? Ziba said, Jonathan still has a son, injured in both feet.
King said, Where is he? Ziba said, Look, he is at house of Machir son of Ammiel, in Lo-debar.
King David sent and took him from house of Machir son of Ammiel, from Lo-debar.
Mephibosheth son of Jonathan son of Saul came to David, fell on his face, and bowed. David said, Mephibosheth. He said, Look, your servant.

David said to him, Do not fear. I will surely do covenant-kindness with you for sake of Jonathan your father. I will restore to you all land of Saul your grandfather, and you shall eat bread at my table continually.
He bowed and said, What is your servant, that you turn toward a dead dog like me?
King called Ziba, servant of Saul, and said, All that belonged to Saul and to all his house I have given to son of your master.

You, your sons, and your servants shall work land for him and bring produce, so son of your master has bread. Yet Mephibosheth son of your master shall eat continually at my table. Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.
Ziba said to king, According to all that my lord king commands his servant, so your servant will do. Mephibosheth shall eat at my table like one of sons of king.
Mephibosheth had a young son named Mica. All who lived in Ziba's house became servants of Mephibosheth.
Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, because he ate continually at table of king. He was injured in both feet.

translation_notes: Mephibosheth's disability is descriptive, not punishment, tragedy shorthand, moral defect, or reason for self-contempt.
His fear before David reflects dynastic danger: new kings often eliminated surviving rival households. 'Do not fear' occurs inside a real power imbalance.
Restored land and table access may express covenant-kindness, but continual residence at the royal table may also function as surveillance or political containment.
Mephibosheth's 'dead dog' language reflects hierarchy and vulnerability, not his actual worth.
Ziba's fifteen sons and twenty servants are assigned agricultural labor by royal command. Their work and consent must not disappear beneath the kindness narrative.
Mephibosheth has a son and a continuing family line; disability does not erase sexuality, parenthood, inheritance, competence, or adulthood.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter for disability pity, inspiration stereotypes, forced gratitude, or claims that powerful patrons own disabled people's loyalty. Covenant care should restore resources and dignity without surveillance, coerced dependence, or unpaid labor.
